planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant after nights stay consistently above 55°F. In zone 9 transplant in February–March and expect harvests from May through October. The plants tolerate heat well and remain productive through the Gulf Coast summer.

directions: Transplant into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; shishito peppers are a slender, thin-walled Japanese variety harvested green at 3–4 inches long, when they are mild and sweet with an occasional — roughly 1 in 10 — surprisingly hot fruit that keeps diners guessing. Plants produce prolifically once they begin fruiting and require consistent moisture and monthly fertilization with a balanced vegetable fertilizer to maintain production. Harvest frequently to keep plants productive; once fruits redden they become hotter and the plant redirects energy away from new fruit. The standard preparation is to blister whole fruits in a dry or lightly oiled cast-iron skillet over high heat until charred and softened, then finish with sea salt and a squeeze of lemon.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pepper Production
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners Database — Shishito

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil and nights have warmed. In zone 9 plant February–March for a spring through fall harvest. Padron peppers are early-season and shorter-lived than many peppers; a second planting in July extends the fall harvest window.

directions: Grow in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil with consistent moisture; Padron peppers are a small, mild-to-medium Spanish variety from the Galicia region of northwestern Spain, famous for the tapas dish Pimientos de Padrón in which they are blistered in olive oil. Like shishito peppers, approximately 1 in 5 fruits carries significant heat, but the majority are mild and sweet when harvested young and small at 1–2 inches. Harvest daily when fruits reach this size; the peppers turn red and become noticeably hotter as they mature. Consistent, frequent harvest keeps plants productive — leaving mature fruits on the plant signals it to slow production.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Peppers
Slow Food USA — Ark of Taste: Pimiento de Padrón

planting.note: Start 10 weeks before last frost; Capsicum baccatum varieties need a long season and slow to germinate below 80°F. In zone 9 start in January for a May transplant; the plants thrive in the heat and remain productive through summer when cooler-season peppers struggle. May overwinter as a perennial in protected zone 9b spots.

directions: Grow in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; aji amarillo is the most essential pepper in Peruvian cuisine, used in virtually every classic dish including ceviche, causa, papa a la huancaína, and aji de gallina. The medium-hot fruits (30,000–50,000 SHU) ripen from green through orange-yellow to a vibrant golden-orange and have a distinctive fruity, raisin-like aroma with tropical fruit undertones unlike any other pepper species. Plants tend to be taller and more vigorous than C. annuum peppers and thrive in the heat and humidity of zone 9 summers. The fresh fruits are rarely available in U.S. markets but frozen or jarred aji amarillo paste is widely used in Peruvian restaurants.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Specialty Peppers for Florida

planting.note: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after nights stay above 55°F. In zone 9 transplant February–March for a spring crop and again in August–September for a fall crop, as the extreme summer heat causes blossom drop. San Marzano is an indeterminate vine requiring sturdy caging or staking.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil with a sturdy cage or stake as San Marzano is a vigorous indeterminate vine reaching 5–6 feet or more. The elongated, plum-shaped fruits with pointed blossom ends are the gold standard for pasta sauce and canned tomatoes — they are low in moisture, dense in flesh, mildly acidic, and have few seeds, making them ideal for reduction into rich sauces. Prune to 2–3 main stems for the largest individual fruits; unpruned plants produce more fruits but smaller. This Italian heirloom from the Campania region near Naples has DOP status in Italy, though the variety sold in the U.S. performs very differently from its Italian counterpart under different soil and climate conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Heirloom Tomato Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Production in Florida
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — San Marzano

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; Jackson Wonder is earlier and more heat-tolerant than most Lima beans, making it the best-adapted Lima for the Gulf Coast and Deep South. In zone 9 plant March–May for spring and again July–August for a fall crop.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Jackson Wonder is a compact, bush-type speckled lima bean that has been the standard Lima for Southern kitchen gardens since the 1880s — it is heat-tolerant where other lima varieties fail to set pods and is the recommended lima bean variety for zone 8–9 gardens. The small-seeded beans are harvested green (fresh shelled) as baby limas at 50–60 days or left to dry on the vine for use as dried beans; the dried seeds are attractively speckled in purple and white. Inoculate seeds with lima bean rhizobia at planting for nitrogen fixation; no fertilization is needed beyond a light dose of phosphorus and potassium at planting.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Jackson Wonder Lima Bean
Alabama Cooperative Extension — Lima Beans for Alabama
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Lima Bean Production
